title: The Son of Mrs. Emma Brown
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in San Diego in June 2016, Harold K. Brown, known as Hal, opens with his birth in York, Pennsylvania, in 1934 as one of seven children of Mrs. Emma Brown, and describes growing up poor in a segregated colored community in a county that was barely one percent Black, attending segregated schools until the seventh grade.

title: The Men in the Factory Windows
summary: Brown recounts the five-mile walk past white schools to his Black elementary school, where men leaned out the upper windows of a chain works factory to hurl racial insults at six-year-old children every day, and jokes that he did not know he was Black until his mother carried him home from the white hospital into the colored community.

title: No Blacks in Positions of Importance
summary: He reflects on the mental toll of seeing only white males in positions of importance, of being the only Black student in an integrated junior high, and links the constant message that he was less than the others to the psychological tests in which Black children chose white dolls as the good and smart ones.

title: You Want to Think About College, Harold
summary: Brown credits the contradictions that pushed back against the racism: his white homeroom and math teacher Cassandra Shea, who adopted him and introduced him to the idea of college and how she had worked through it cleaning houses, and his football coach who told him he ought to be a dentist, a profession he had never seen a Black person hold.

title: Old Black Joe Every Day
summary: He remembers the only interracial images at school being slaves picking cotton, which made Black students sink down in their seats, and a music teacher who sat at the piano and made the class sing Old Black Joe seemingly every day, learning nothing of Black history until after he graduated from college.

title: All-State, in His Brother's Footsteps
summary: Brown describes becoming a popular high-school athlete, named all-state basketball player his senior year after his brother had been the first in their school's history to earn that status, attending Penn State on a basketball scholarship that paid only tuition, and leaving to take a tryout with the St. Louis Browns in Thomasville, Georgia.

title: Drafted Between Two Schools
summary: He recounts being declared ineligible as a transfer at San Diego State and steered to a junior college that would have cost him his eligibility, the lack of any guiding adult in his life, and a baseball tryout where, unprepared and out of shape from a Pennsylvania winter, his arm gave out before he was drafted into the Army.

title: Friendships the Sports Allowed
summary: Brown reflects on athletics as one of the few avenues for cross-racial friendship, noting that the relationships he built with white teammates rarely extended beyond the field or the school, that Black players socialized together at the Crispus Attucks community center, and that even in college the teams left much to be desired.

title: No Jews or Niggers Allowed
summary: He tells of inquiring about his draft status on a visit to Pennsylvania in 1953, being inducted at the end of the Korean War, and doing basic training at Camp Gordon, Georgia, where life on post was tolerable but the town displayed signs reading no dogs, Jews, or niggers allowed, so the Black soldiers stayed on base.

title: I'm Not Going Down There
summary: Stationed at Fort Monmouth, New Jersey, Brown describes the one trouble there: an assignment to Operation Sagebrush in Louisiana, which he refused by walking in to tell the post commander an enlisted man should not be sent where he was not wanted, only to be sent to the outskirts of Shreveport with everyone else.

title: I Couldn't Approve That
summary: Back at San Diego State after the Army, Brown recounts winning a student-council seat and chairing the Constitution Committee, where he found the white sororities and fraternities had charters restricting membership to white males or white females and refused in good conscience to approve them, drawing a meeting with the dean of students.

title: For Me It Was Anger
summary: He describes being one of about twenty-five Black students in an enrollment near seven thousand, the loneliness of always being the only Black person in the classroom, and how being called a slur on a San Diego street fed an anger that, as he matured, led him to decide on graduation to join the civil rights movement.

title: They Don't Hire Blacks to Coach
summary: Brown did postgraduate work in speech pathology and aimed to be a physical-education teacher and coach, only to realize that high schools did not hire Black coaches, and when he applied to teach in La Mesa the superintendent said the only way to hire him was to poll all the district's teachers about accepting a Negro.

title: A Chapter of CORE in San Diego
summary: He recounts joining the all-white El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee, being approached around 1960 by a Black man about forming a San Diego chapter of CORE, and landing a teaching job through a San Diego State schoolmate, a post he held for five years before the Peace Corps, while at first he knew nothing about CORE.
keywords: El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee

title: Cool It the First Year
summary: Brown explains deciding to prove himself as a teacher before becoming active, being named one of San Diego's five outstanding new teachers, and recalls that during the 1955 Montgomery bus boycott he was still in the Army at Fort Monmouth, following the civil rights news on television though he never saw a newspaper on base.

title: The Afro-American Association
summary: He describes how the chapter wrote its bylaws and held elections, the tension over his prior membership in the Afro-American Association (labeled a Black nationalist group by the media) that taught Black history and the plight of Black people, and his first reading of Lerone Bennett Jr., which began filling his library.

title: Nineteen Segregated Schools
summary: As chairman Brown found deplorable conditions: nineteen segregated schools, but employment discrimination as the top priority, with Black people unable to be hired even as grocery baggers or downtown workers, recalling earlier demonstrations against Woolworth's led by the Black dentist Dr. Jack Kimbrough.

title: We Don't Rent to Negroes
summary: Brown turns to housing, where Black people could not rent or buy outside Logan Heights, and tells how the woman he married in 1967, a math major recruited from Columbus, Ohio by General Dynamics as a computer programmer, was turned away from apartment after apartment and had to stay at the Y, which is how he met her while she was buying a car.

title: A Hundred Friends in the Pool
summary: He recounts helping his future wife find an apartment with a pool whose manager refused, blaming an owner in Canada, and how Brown first proposed knocking on every tenant's door to ask if they objected to a Negro neighbor, then threatened to bring a hundred Black friends to swim in the pool, at which the manager suddenly found a vacancy.

title: My Jail Sentences Came From the Bank
summary: Brown describes covenants and demonstrations against the California Real Estate Association, and the biggest project: a statewide campaign as Western Region chairman of CORE against Bank of America and San Diego Gas and Electric to open employment to Black workers, which earned him his jail sentences, and his friendships with national CORE leaders James Farmer and Floyd McKissick.

title: Counting Pennies at the Teller Window
summary: He details the tactics: marches and sit-ins, mail-ins that sent bank payments over or under the balance as a nuisance, and coin-ins where members brought sacks of pennies to the teller window and counted them out one at a time while others sat on the floor singing, tactics he calls very effective in forcing the companies to start hiring.

title: We Laid Our Jobs on the Line
summary: Brown recalls being summoned to Sacramento before the state board to explain why his teaching credential should not be revoked over his CORE leadership, going with a CORE lawyer to defend himself, keeping the credential, and serving his jail time on weekends so he could keep teaching, calling it an intimidating, scary time.

title: More White Members Than Black
summary: He reflects that the San Diego chapter was about two-thirds white, that recruiting Black members was hard work because many Black people were afraid of reprisals and unwilling to risk what little they had, and that some friends would not even admit to their white acquaintances that they knew him.

title: The NAACP I Couldn't Find
summary: Brown discusses the Black community's origins through the Navy and migration from Texas, a settlement of Negroes in Ramona, the small Latino population then in Logan Heights, and the other organizations: an NAACP so invisible he tried and failed to find its office, an Urban League present but uninvolved, and the fading Afro-American Association.

title: Fighting on Two Fronts
summary: He describes a less-known struggle: fighting white racism outside while battling infiltration inside, with the peace movement seeking influence he refused, and accusations of communism and Trotskyism he could not even identify, all part of a campaign to oust him and take over the chapter's leadership.

title: More Painful Than the Companies
summary: Brown calls the internal fighting more painful than the fight against the discriminating companies because it came from people inside the organization, both Black and white, and recounts that after his jail sentences he resigned around 1967, after which CORE faded and the conflict between the Black Panthers and the US Organization that followed in San Diego was only hearsay to him.

title: A Room Above the Airport
summary: Brown turns to the Peace Corps, where he served as deputy director in Lesotho, a country ringed by South Africa, and tells of being held with his wife in a room above the Johannesburg airport reserved for Africans under apartheid, deciding to test it by walking the airport until an embassy man quietly checked that he was all right.

title: The Peace Corps Was Black to Them
summary: He recounts landing in Lesotho under Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan to a joyous welcome, the shock of the Basotho when the white Peace Corps volunteers later arrived, his speaking engagements in Maseru where people asked how Black Americans were treated, and the Charge d'Affaires displeasure that he told the truth and exposed America.

title: The King Walked In
summary: Brown describes how the assassination of Martin Luther King was too much to bear from outside the country, leading him to organize a memorial service at the AME church in Lesotho, where the house-arrested King Moshoeshoe II, who had opposed apartheid, walked in to ululation and sat through the whole service as Brown gave the remarks and his wife sang.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

part: The Long Way Back to San Diego State
title: McKissick Enterprises and the Bank
summary: He recounts returning to the States, being unable to find a San Diego position, joining Floyd McKissick at McKissick Enterprises in Harlem, then working as a loan officer at Marine Midland Bank on Wall Street, until an old San Diego acquaintance called offering a post as assistant to the president at San Diego State to help start a Black studies program.

title: The First Black Administrator
summary: Brown describes starting at San Diego State in September 1971 as the university's first Black administrator, hiring newly minted PhDs, establishing a major and minor and winning the major coup of getting the courses to satisfy general-education requirements, then leaving after two years for the directorship of computer services, with the program now the full-fledged Africana Studies department.

title: The Black Economic Development Task Force
summary: He traces his later path: associate dean in the College of Business Administration until retiring in 2004 after twenty-six years, then real estate development, and the 1987 founding of the Black Economic Development Task Force and a community economic development certificate program, which the university kept running because of him until he retired.

title: Ninety Percent of the Work Is You
summary: Brown reflects that his programs could not pay for themselves the way Columbia or NYU programs could, that a Leadership Training Program he founded after CORE drew praise even from the Labor Department before fading when he left for the Peace Corps, and that in nonprofit work he founded, ninety percent of the work fell to him.

title: Community Leaders Undoing Biases
summary: He describes forming an organization at the business school, the acronym CLUB for Community Leaders Undoing Biases, that brought prominent San Diego multimillionaires together monthly over lunch to discuss race, and how after ten years he tired and no one took his place, defending himself against the charge that he failed to delegate by asking, delegate to whom.

title: How Do You Get to That Point
summary: Asked what should happen now, Brown says we must learn the lessons of the past, including the old fight within CORE over whether integration belonged in its constitution, and frames the question driving his planned book: how a mild, shy athlete became willing to sacrifice his life and career, militant though not insane.

title: How We Fit Into Capitalism
summary: Brown argues the movement, busy kicking doors open for jobs and education, never addressed how Black Americans fit into capitalism, and that even first-class citizenship is not enough; he began years ago thinking about building wealth, which is why he founded the economic-development efforts, insisting that the word capital matters and that people must learn to save.

title: They Couldn't Put Those Two Words Together
summary: He recalls that in 1987 Black people could barely join the words Black and economics, that many were taught wealth is bad, and that a chief obstacle is never having learned to work together outside the church, urging a real focus on saving and on understanding the capital that real estate development and other ventures require.

title: We Hit a Wall
summary: Brown observes that after the civil rights movement Black people hit a wall, that some got good jobs like his own while poverty, lack of education, and the criminal justice system persist, and that Black Americans became great spenders rather than generators of jobs, tying this to a Georgia activist's definition of Black power as economic power and self-determination.

title: They Have to See It Happen
summary: He explains that self-determination motivated him to refuse the white-led peace movement and Trotskyites who wanted to impose their will on CORE, and that after retiring he went into real estate and land development to make an example, so children could see wealth built not by an athlete or movie star but by a man like himself.

title: How Much Did Your Shoes Cost
summary: Brown relates how his son, who played basketball for Stanford, found kids asking only about the cost of his shoes and his car, and laments that even his accomplished fraternity brothers, doctors and lawyers and educators, had no grounding in business or economics, which he had urged through Black economic summits since the 1980s as the movement's next step.

title: CORE Had a Voice
summary: He observes that in the CORE days the city fathers heeded the organization but now the Black community has no voice, recounts meeting the San Diego Urban League's CEO and assembling a think tank of men to advise him, and notes the same need across the Latino, Filipino, and Asian communities.

title: Forced Through the Schools
summary: Asked whether Black Lives Matter or the Bernie Sanders movement give him hope, Brown answers no, says change requires anger and commotion, hoped Black studies programs would force the lessons but sees no sign of it, and notes that San Diegans his own age do not even know there was a civil rights movement in San Diego.

title: Stuck in Its Corner
topic: Black Studies for the Whole University
summary: Brown recalls speaking to a 1972 San Diego State class that knew nothing of the movement, his goal of marketing the African American Studies program to the whole university rather than only Black students, his being ousted over internal matters, and his belief that figures like Jesse Jackson and John Lewis could tell of the infighting that limited the movement's progress.

title: My Nervous System Was Shot
summary: Brown discloses that when he left CORE his nervous system was completely shot, close to a nervous breakdown, likens it to the post-traumatic stress of soldiers returning from war, notes activists who were torn apart and ended up strung out on drugs, and returns to his conviction that the answer is to get economic-minded.

title: Not a Word About Economics
topic: A Closing Plea for Economic Leadership
summary: Brown praises the broadcaster Tony Brown, whose KPBS program preached Black business and wealth and showed how little Black people spent in their own communities, laments that leaders like John Lewis and Jesse Jackson never spoke of economics, and closes wishing he could press the point on Hillary Clinton, before the project's standard sign-off.
